name: Lemmino
video_title: Shouting at Stars: A History of Interstellar Messages
transcript:
In early 1972, a probe known as
Pioneer 10 was launched into space on a mission to the largest
planet in the Solar System. After millennia of
speculation and wonder, humanity got its first
close-up view of Jupiter. Accelerated by the
immense gravity of the planet, Pioneer 10 was then
catapulted into deep space, destined to leave the Solar System
and roam the galaxy for billions of years. On the off-chance that this
vestige of humanity should one day be intercepted
by an alien intelligence, it was affixed with a message. A message designed to answer
three central questions. [Carl Sagan]
Where we are, when we are, and who we are. More than half a century
has now passed since the launch of our
first galactic emissary, and in that time, we've made numerous
attempts to communicate with life beyond. Either by attaching greeting cards
to probes headed for interstellar space or by targeting our cosmic neighbors
with high-powered beams of light. So far, humanity's been left on read,
but that could change at any moment. As long as we keep making noise,
there's always a chance we'll be heard. An exceedingly slim chance, as we'll see,
but a non-zero chance nonetheless. Given the paradigm-shifting potential
of contacting beings from another world, it sure would be nice to know what exactly
we've been telling them all these years. I mean, don't get me wrong, "Where we are, when we are,
and who we are", sounds really neat and all,
but it's not exactly rich in detail. So let's start with
that plaque on Pioneer 10. What does it say,
and more importantly, to whom should I direct my anger when
the aliens arrive to harvest my bones? The idea to attach
a message to Pioneer 10 was the brainchild of journalist
and author Eric Burgess. Less than four months before
the probe was scheduled to launch, Burgess approached famed astronomer
and science communicator Carl Sagan, who, in turn, relayed
the proposal to NASA. Sagan was given three
weeks to devise the message, and with the help of
his wife and artist Linda as well as fellow
astronomer Frank Drake, they scrambled together
a series of pictograms etched into a gold-laden
plate of aluminum. In the words of Sagan... Near the upper-left corner,
we find a diagram of hydrogen, the simplest and most abundant
element in the universe. One electron
bound to a proton. More specifically, the diagram
depicts a single atom of hydrogen transitioning from one
energy state to another, which is meant to be conveyed by this
horizontal line here in the middle. This shift in energy is known
as a hyperfine transition, and it's accompanied
by the emission of a radio wave. That radio wave has
a very specific frequency that's so prominent across the universe
that we've given it a name, the hydrogen line. Something that beings
of sufficient intelligence to capture a
drifting spacecraft would no doubt
be aware of as well. In human terms, it has a frequency
of about 1420 megahertz which means that each cycle of the wave
has a period of about 0.7 nanoseconds and a corresponding length
of roughly 21 centimeters. The recipients would obviously
express their measurements using something other than
nanoseconds and centimeters, but what's important here is that
they're measuring the same thing and that that thing
never changes. The duration of one wavecycle and
the distance covered during that cycle remains the same
across the universe. This allows us to establish a
shared unit of both time and length. We're basically telling the
aliens to treat their measurement of the hyperfine transition
of neutral hydrogen as one unit of time
and one unit of length. That's why there's a vertical
line here in the middle, which is meant to convey
the concept of one in binary. But, of course, hydrogen
is merely one of many possible interpretations
of this diagram. The aliens could just as easily
think it's some sort of language, technology, star system,
or literally anything else. To help them narrow down the list, the
plaque includes three outlined silhouettes of a man, a woman,
and Pioneer 10. These illustrations
were drawn to scale, so the aliens can use the
spacecraft's diameter as a basis for deriving the height
of the two figures in the foreground. In human terms, that would make
the man approximately 182 centimeters and the woman about 169. The woman's height has
been further emphasized by two demarcation marks
along the right edge here. Between them is a sequence of four lines
representing the number eight in binary. The purpose of this number
is to communicate to the aliens that the woman is eight times as tall
as the wavelength of the hydrogen line. In human terms,
that's about 169, just like the woman's height
relative to the probe's diameter. In other words, the interpretation
of this as a schematic of hydrogen and the interpretation of this
as an outline of the spacecraft are mutually corroborative. They provide the aliens with a means
of deriving the height of this figure on the basis of two
distinct measurements, which should help them narrow
down the list of possible interpretations. At least, that was the idea. But when you actually measure
the height of the couple relative to this
sketch of the probe, you quickly realize they're
about 10 centimeters too tall. While I have no way of confirming this,
I suspect the source of this discrepancy is that the reflector dish on
Pioneer 10 is not a perfect circle. It's slightly wider in this
dimension than in this one, known as a major
and minor axis. If we assume that this
outline of the probe is oriented with the minor
axis along the vertical, the relative height of the couple
is indeed what you'd expect. But if that is what the
Sagans and Drake intended, only two of the three antenna struts
should be visible here in the front. The fact that all three have
been drawn on the plaque, implies the probe is oriented with
the major axis along the vertical, thereby increasing the couples'
height by roughly 10 centimeters. Nevertheless, it's hardly surprising for
an oversight like this to go unnoticed given they only had three weeks
to assemble everything, and Sagan acknowledged
they could have done a better job had they not been
so pressed for time. Moving on to the
left side of the plaque, we find a starburst pattern of 15 straight
lines emanating from a central point. This is a galactic
map of pulsars that's meant to help the aliens
pinpoint the Solar System. Pulsars are rapidly rotating neutron
stars that can easily be spotted from thousands or even
millions of lightyears away. They're often described
as cosmic clocks or lighthouses because they're
not only extremely bright but pulsate at very
consistent rates. This red line shooting
off to the right represents the distance between the Sun
and the center of the galaxy, while the green lines
represent the relative distance between the Sun and 14 pulsars. To make it easier to
identify the pulsars, each line is accompanied by the star's
rotation period expressed in binary, marked here in blue. Take this one,
for instance. This star is known as the Vela Pulsar,
and it's about 1 000 lightyears from Earth. Its rotation period has been expressed
in waveperiods of the hydrogen line and reads 126 726 823 when
translated from binary to decimal. What that means is that
it takes the Vela Pulsar almost 127 million times
longer to complete one rotation than the waveperiod of a hydrogen
atom undergoing hyperfine transition. In human terms, that's about
89 million nanoseconds or eleven rotations every second,
which is about this fast. By scouring the
galaxy for pulsars that match the information
embedded within this map, the aliens could theoretically
triangulate the Solar System. In fact, the chart not only discloses
our location in space but also in time. As Sagan put it... [Carl Sagan]
Where we are, when we are, and who we are. This goes back to the
cosmic clock analogy because pulsars gradually wind
down at highly predictable rates. So even though the Vela Pulsar
will have a much slower rate of spin by the time it's observed
by our hypothetical aliens, they could simply wind back
the clock until its rotation period matched the one
etched into the plaque. Repeating that process for
at least some of the other pulsars would enable them to figure
out not just from where, but also when Pioneer 10
was launched. At least, that was the idea. But when the plaque
was created, the existence of pulsars had only
been known for about four years. Astronomers have since
learned a lot more about them, and they're not nearly as rare, stable,
and long-lived as once believed. They can only be observed from
very specific vantage points, and unpredictable speedups,
slowdowns, and glitches challenge the
cosmic clock analogy. Sagan and Drake were
aware of at least some of these issues, but there
was only so much they could've foreseen with the limited data
available at the time. In fact, there was
such great uncertainty regarding the distance of
some of the featured pulsars that they added these
breaks in the lines, indicating they're based on rough
estimates rather than exact figures. Finally, on the
bottom of the plaque, we find a plain schematic
of the Solar System. These binary numbers represent
the orbital distance of each planet expressed as multiples of one tenth of the
mean distance between the Sun and Mercury. For instance, the number above
Earth reads 26 in decimal, which means it is
26 times further away than 10% of the distance
between the Sun and Mercury. The obvious problem
with this approach is that it requires the recipients to
already be familiar with the Solar System. Otherwise, one tenth of the distance
between the Sun and Mercury is completely meaningless. We might as well have expected them
to be familiar with kilometers or miles. To quote the creators of the plaque... It's unclear why the
Sagans and Drake knowingly used an
indecipherable unit of length when they had already gone through the
trouble of creating a universal yardstick with this schematic of hydrogen. My best guess is that the wavelength
of a state-shifting hydrogen atom was simply too small a unit to
represent interplanetary distances, and the resulting binary strings became
too long to neatly fit on the plaque. Again, they only had three
weeks to do all of this. Last but not least, the trajectory of the
spacecraft has been traced with an arrow, and the probe's main antenna
is pointing at home. Over half a century later, Pioneer 10 is now more than
20 billion kilometers from the Sun. That might sound like a lot, especially
given how I just emphasized that number, but it's only about three
times further away than Pluto, and it takes less than a day for a
light beam to cover the same distance. Even though it's darting through
space at several kilometers per second, the probe won't surpass even
the nearest star to the Sun for many tens of
thousands of years. But it gets worse because Pioneer 10
is not headed for any particular star. It's expected to pass within a few trillion
kilometers of a relatively nearby system in about 90 000 years or so
when this star, all the way out here, will come closer to the Sun
than Proxima Centauri is today. While an exoplanet has yet
to be detected around this star, if one does exist, and that planet
just so happens to be inhabited by intelligent beings in possession of
technology that allows them to detect a perfectly silent and practically invisible
speck gently gliding by their system at a distance greater than
a thousand times the orbit of Pluto, then, yeah... ...maybe they'll see it. A nearly identical probe was launched
in 1973 known as Pioneer 11, and it was outfitted with the exact
same plaque as Pioneer 10. Much like its predecessor, Pioneer 11
was just kind of hurtled into deep space with no particular
target in mind. While a chance encounter with another
planetary system is not impossible, even across timescales
of many billions of years, Sagan and Drake believed
it was more likely for the probes

name: Practical Engineering
video_title: The Los Angeles Aqueduct is Wild
transcript:
On the northern edge of Los Angeles, fresh water 
spills down two stark concrete chutes perched on   the foothills of the San Gabriel Mountains, 
a place simply called The Cascades. It’s a   deceptively simple-looking finish line: 
the end of a roughly 300-mile (or 500   km) journey from the eastern slopes 
of the Sierra Nevada into the city. On November 5, 1913, tens of thousands of people 
climbed these hills to watch the first water   arrive. When the gates finally opened, water 
trickled through, but that trickle quickly   became a torrent. The project’s chief engineer, 
William Mulholland, leaned over to the mayor and   shouted the line that’s been repeated ever 
since: “There it is, Mr. Mayor. Take it!” That moment was profound for a lot of reasons, 
depending on where you live and how you feel   about water rights. LA didn’t become LA 
by living within the limits of its local   resources. Its meteoric growth into the metropolis 
we know was enabled by an early and extraordinary   decision to reach far beyond its own watershed 
and pull a whole new river into town. Today,   roughly a third of LA’s water comes from the 
Eastern Sierra through the Los Angeles Aqueduct   system. That share swings with snowpack, 
drought, and environmental constraints,   but this one piece of infrastructure helped turn 
a water-limited town into a world city. It’s one   of the most impressive and controversial 
engineering projects in American history. But to really appreciate that water 
in the cascades, you have to look way   upstream and see what it took to get it 
there. It’s gravity, geology, politics,   and human ambition all in a part of the 
state that most people never see. Let’s   take a little tour so you can see what I mean. 
I’m Grady and this is Practical Engineering. When most people think about aqueducts, this is 
what they picture:  a bridge carrying water over a valley or river. And, just to be clear, these are aqueducts. But engineers often use the term   more broadly to describe any type of conveyance 
system that carries water over a long distance   from a source to a distribution point. Could be 
a canal, a pipe, a tunnel, or even just a ditch.   In the case of the LA aqueduct, it’s all of them, 
plus a lot of supporting infrastructure as well. From the center of the city, it’s about a four 
hour drive to the Owens River Diversion Weir. It’s   not accessible to the public, but it is 
the official start of the LA Aqueduct,   at least when it was originally built. Here, all 
the snowmelt and rain from a huge drainage system   between the Sierra Nevada and Inyo Mountains 
funnel down into the Owens River, where a large   concrete diversion weir peels nearly all of it out 
of its natural course and into a canal. This point   is roughly 2,500 feet (or 750 meters) higher 
in elevation than the bottom of the Cascades   at the downstream end, which makes it obvious why 
LA chose it as a source. The entire aqueduct is a   gravity machine. There are no pumps pushing the 
water toward the city. Half a mile of elevation   change feels like a lot until you realize you 
have to spread it out over 300 miles. It’s   all achieved through careful grading and managing 
elevations along the way to keep the flow moving. That care is particularly important 
in this upper section of the aqueduct,   where the water flows in an open 
canal. To do this efficiently,   you need a relatively constant slope from 
start to finish. That’s a tough thing to   achieve on the surface of a bumpy earth. 
Following a river valley makes this easier,   but you can see the twists and turns necessary to 
keep the aqueduct on its gentle slope toward LA. If it seems kind of wild that a city 
would buy up the land and water rights   from somewhere so far away, it did to a lot of 
the people who lived in the Owens Valley, too.   A lot of the acquisitions and politics of 
the original LA Aqueduct were carried out   in bad faith, souring relationships 
with landowners, ranchers, farmers,   and communities in the area. The saga is full 
of broken promises and shady dealings. Then   when the diversion started, the area dried 
up, disrupting the ecology of the region,   making agriculture more difficult and residents 
even more resentful.  Many resorted to violence, not against people but against the infrastructure. They vandalized parts of the aqueduct, a conflict   that later became known as the California 
Water Wars. In one case in 1924, ranchers   used dynamite to blow up a part of the canal. 
Later that year, they seized the Alabama Gates. About 20 miles or 35 kilometers 
downstream from the diversion weir,   a set of gates sits on the eastern bank of 
the aqueduct canal. Because it runs beside   the river valley, the aqueduct captures 
some of the water that flows down from   the surrounding mountains in addition to 
what’s diverted out of the Owens River,   particularly during strong storms. That means it’s 
actually possible for the canal to overfill. The   Alabama Gates serve as a spillway, allowing 
operators to divert water back down to the   river. This also helps drain the canal 
for maintenance or repairs when needed. Those Owens Valley ranchers understood exactly 
what the Alabama Gates controlled. Open them,   and the water would run back where it 
had always run, down the Owens River,   instead of south to Los Angeles. The resistance 
simmered and flared for years, but it didn’t   end in the dramatic showdown at the aqueduct. 
Instead, it ended at a bank counter. The Inyo   County Bank was run by two brothers who were also 
key organizers and financiers of the resistance   campaign. In August 1927, an audit revealed 
major shortfalls and ongoing embezzlement,   and the bank quickly collapsed. Residents 
across the valley saw their savings wiped out   or frozen overnight, shattering what was left 
of the community’s ability to keep fighting. The Alabama Gates weren’t just a political 
flashpoint though. They also marked an   important dividing line in the aqueduct’s design. 
LA knew that even if the ranchers didn’t release   the water to the river in protests, a lot of 
it would end up there anyway through seepage.   As the canal climbed away from the valley floor 
and crossed more porous soil, it would naturally   lose its water through the ground. So, at the 
Alabama Gates, the aqueduct transitions from an   unlined canal to a concrete-lined channel. It’s 
still open to the air, so there’s no protection   against evaporation or contamination, but 
the losses to the ground are a lot less. This design continues for about 35 miles (or 55 
kilometers) through the valley. Along the way,   the aqueduct passes the remains of Owens Lake. 
Once a large body of water, it quickly dried   up with the diversion of the Owens River. Of 
course, there were impacts to wildlife from the   loss of water, but the bigger 
problem came later: dust.  All the fine sediment that settled on 
the lakebed over thousands of years was now exposed to the hot desert sun. 
When the wind picked up,  it filled the air with fine particulates that 
are dangerous to breathe. Over the years,   there have been times when Owens Lake is the 
single largest source of dust pollution in   the entire country, and LA has spent more than a 
billion dollars just trying to fix this problem   alone. The aqueduct passing along the hillside 
past the lake and its challenges is a reminder   that the true cost of water is often a lot more 
than the infrastructure it takes to deliver it. So far, it might be obvious that this aqueduct 
system is pretty fragile to be making up a   major part of a city’s fresh water supply. Even 
beyond the vandalism and political resistance,   there are a lot of things that could go wrong 
along the way, from bank collapses, earthquakes,   diversion failures, and more. That’s why Haiwee 
Reservoir was originally built in a narrow saddle   between two hills as a kind of buffer. With a dam 
on either side, it stored water up so the aqueduct   could keep running even during a disruption 
upstream. It also slowed the water down, exposing   it to the hot desert sun as a natural form of 
UV disinfection. In the 1960s, the reservoir   was reconfigured into two basins to add some 
flexibility. That’s because, around that time,   the LA aqueduct became two. While the open-topped 
canal section was large enough to meet demands,   the underground conduit in the next section 
wasn’t. So, LA built a second one in 1970 to   increase the flow. If you look at this map of the 
Haiwee Reservoirs, you can see that water has two   paths: it can flow into the second aqueduct here 
from the north basin, or it can pass through the   Merritt Cut to the south reservoir, through 
the intake there, and into the first aqueduct.   This setup allows for some redundancy, 
along with regulation and balancing of the   flows between the two aqueducts. Haiwee marks 
the start of the long desert run, with both   systems no longer in open-topped lined canals, 
but running underground in concrete conduits. There are a lot of advantages to running an 
aqueduct in a closed conduit underground,   especially one this long through a desert 
landscape. There’s far less evaporation and less   potential for contamination. It doesn’t divide the

transcript: Some massive companies are set to go public very soon. Private companies like SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic would rank among the largest companies if they went public and index funds would be forced to buy their stock. That means that if you are an index fund investor, you may soon be investing in these companies whether you like it or not. The problem is that most IPOs are terrible investments and these ones in particular raise some questions. I'm Ben Felix, chief investment officer at PWL Capital, and I'm going to tell you what the upcoming mega IPOs mean for you and your portfolio. In this video, I'm going to take you through how stock indices and the index funds tracking them include IPOs, how this might affect index fund returns, and what you can do about it. I'll also cover whether index fund investors are missing out on private market returns, which is a common perception based on companies staying private longer. Index funds are generally great investments because they roughly deliver the returns that the public stock market has to offer. Since their primary role is representing the stock market, some stock indices aim to include new IPOs soon after the company goes public. This makes sense for the purpose of representing the public stock market. But the problem for index fund investors is that IPOs have had historically terrible returns. I'll come back to that point later. Index funds today control trillions of dollars. So, if you're a new company listing on the stock market or an investment bank facilitating an IPO, getting the stock included in a major index or multiple indices can mean huge investment dollars flowing into its shares on the public market as index funds are forced to buy, giving sellers liquidity and pushing up the share price. This makes index inclusion very desirable for shareholders of the newly listed company, but probably not so much for the index fund investors who are left holding the bag. And it is often a bag that they are left holding. Companies tend to go public when they think they can sell their stock at a high price. Meaning, the moment you can buy their stock on the secondary market is precisely when the insiders believe it's overvalued or at least nicely valued. If you're an investor, you probably don't want to buy an overpriced stock, but index funds don't have that kind of discretion. They're forced to gobble up whatever is included in the index, regardless of its price. The IPO inclusion rules vary across indices. For example, as of right now, the S&P 500 requires a stock to have been trading on a public exchange for 12 months before inclusion, while the S&P total market index allows for inclusion within 5 days for stocks that meet certain criteria. This is called fasttrack entry which is important to understand. Basically stocks that meet certain criteria can be eligible for near immediate inclusion in some total market indices. As I mentioned, the S&P 500 has historically required shares to trade for a year before inclusion. But as reported by Bloomberg, S&P may be considering an S&P 500 rule change to accelerate the inclusion of mega IPOs like SpaceX. NASDAQ is considering similar changes for the NASDAQ 100 to accelerate inclusion. The implications here for index fund investors are important. A 2025 paper looks at how fast track entry into Crisp, that Center for Research and Security Prices Indices affects stock returns and IPO deal structure. VTI, for example, a large ETF, tracks the CRISP US total market index. Crisp adds eligible securities to the index within as few as 5 days for fasttrack entry. The authors of this paper show that expected index investor demand for IPO shares causes FastTrack IPOs to outperform their non-fastrack counterparts by over 5 percentage points following their listing. This outperformance peaks at the index inclusion date and reverts significantly within two weeks thereafter. Basically, index funds are being frontr run by intermediaries like hedge funds who know that index funds are going to be buying the shares once they become eligible for index inclusion and then the index funds end up holding the shares as they revert back down closer to their IPO price. The authors call this a shadow tax largely paid by index fund investors. It's like ticket scalpers for concerts and sports events. Another important index definition that's proving to be relevant to mega IPOs is the concept of free float. Free float is the proportion of a company's shares that are available for purchase in the public equity market. Most major indices have a minimum float requirement and weight stocks by their public float. A company could go public while only making a small portion of their overall market capitalization freely available on the public exchange. This would be referred to as a lowflat IPO. As reported by the Financial Times, SpaceX plans to float less than 5% of its equity, much lower than the average IPO. With a $ 1.75 trillion valuation, but only a 5% public float, most indices would weight it at its float of 88 billion, and many would actually exclude it altogether. NASDAQ currently has a 10% minimum float and weight stocks based on the value of all listed shares of the company with no regard for free free float, which is unique. But they recently approved rule changes following a public consultation to speed up IPO inclusion, eliminate the lowflat cutoff, and introduce a float factor in their waiting of lowflat stocks. The cynical view on this is that NASDAQ is changing the NASDAQ 100 index rules to win SpaceX over in an effort to get their listing on the NASDAQ exchange. including in the index would force significant index fund buying, which likely ends up being good for SpaceX, its early investors, and NASDAQ, but potentially comes at the expense of NASDAQ 100 index fund investors. Some indices like the crisp US total market index tracked by VTI continue to have a low float cutoff of 10% for fasttrack entry into the index. Between inclusion criteria and index waiting methodologies, there's some uncertainty about how these mega IPOs will initially show up in indices. But there's no question that their listings will change the public market over time. That's likely why index providers are considering making changes to get these companies represented in their indices. To reiterate a point from earlier, indices are meant to represent the stock market. A blog post from index provider S&P Global shows that just SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic would make up 2.9% of the S&P World Index at their full market caps. That's almost as much as Canada. But these numbers assume that the total market cap of the companies is included in the index. Since many of these IPOs are expected to have low public floats, that should be accounted for in estimating their market impact. In another blog post, index provider MSCI calculated the potential changes to the MSCI allcountry world investable market index in the event of some of the largest private companies going public in 2026. This was written back in February 2026 when SpaceX had a quaint valuation of only 800 billion, but the general points are still relevant. They ask in the blog post what would happen to the index to this world index if the 10 largest companies from the Msei all country venturebacked private company index went public. Since the free float at the time of listing is unknown, they go through a few scenarios. At a 5% float, only four of the 10 large private companies would achieve index inclusion based on MSCI's inclusion rules. At a 10% float, seven of the 10 companies would achieve index inclusion, but not at enormous weights in the index. While the overall market weight changes in percentage terms would not be huge, even at a 25% float scenario, MSEI finds that the dollar flows just for funds tracking MSCI indices would be huge with newly listed private companies receiving billions in investor dollars and the largest existing public companies seeing billions in outflows. These flows are what ultimately matter to index fund investors who are being forced to buy shares. Okay, so index funds, depending on the rules of the specific indices they track, may or may not invest in these mega IPOs. An important piece of understanding this whole situation is the empirical fact that investing in IPOs is one of the worst investment strategies that exists. Before I get to that though, make sure to subscribe if you haven't already. I make nerdy research-driven videos to help you make more informed financial decisions. Okay, IPOs tend to have a first day pop where the price on the public market jumps up relative to the IPO price, but most investors don't get the IPO price. Investing in the shares once they are listed on the public market, so after the pop has been rough to say the least. The consistent pattern of IPO underperformance even has a name. It's the new issues puzzle coined in a highly cited 1995 paper which found that companies issuing stock from 1970 to 1990 tended to be poor investments. The study found that these poor returns were largely explained by the factors in the FM and French five factor asset pricing model. The IPO portfolio behaves like a portfolio of small growth, lowprofitability, high investment stocks, also known as junk or small crap growth. These types of stocks are more volatile and tend to lag behind the broader market. We don't even need these papers though. These findings are easy for anyone to replicate and update today because of course there is an ETF for IPOs. It has underperformed VTI which tracks the total US market by more than six percentage points annualized since inception in October 2013. And as expected, it behaves like a portfolio of small stocks with high prices, low profitability, and aggressive asset growth. Similar results can be found internationally since 2014 with the Renaissance International IPO ETF IPOS. Yes, that is the real ticker. Professor Jay Ritterder, co-author of the new issues puzzle, maintains an up-to-date database of IPO returns. In that data spanning 1980 through 2023, the average three-year buy and hold return for IPOs purchased on the secondary market trails the market by 19 percentage points. Lowflat IPOs look particularly bad. its usually augmented with various refractory   sands to create a solid layer of ceramic.
There are several choices of sand available,   and two commonly used are zircon 
sand in a fine mesh, and a coarser   refractory material known as chamotte.
Zircon is a hard mineral sand with a   natural heat resistance which makes 
it perfect for the first metal facing   layers. Depending on the pattern, I generally use 
zircon sand for up to 5 of the initial layers.  Chamotte is a fairly light, coarse material, 
suitable for the remaining layers. This coarser   material functions as a filler to bulk up 
the mold for greater structural strength,   and to give it additional thermal mass.
Now with resin printed models, a wetting   agent is often required to ensure the first coat 
of slurry fully adheres to the pattern. I’m using   shellac flakes, something like 200 grams, 
dissolved in 5 liters of denatured alcohol.  Once dry, the pattern is ready 
for the first investment cycle.  The investment process benefits greatly from 
being conducted as a standardised routine,   to help achieve consistency across all layers.
My routine is to first re-suspend the slurry,   and then position it alongside the sand 
that I’m about to apply, along with a   wash bucket, and a source of compressed air.
The pattern is slowly dipped into the slurry.  And a brush serves to help cover 
any sections missed in the dip.  A brush can also help push the slurry 
into any deeper cavities in the pattern,   and remove surface bubbles.
Occasional soft bursts with   compressed air helps to remove the bubbles too.
Then as best as possible, the pattern is kept in   continuous motion, as the excess slurry 
drips off and back into the bucket.  Once the drips have mostly stopped, the sand is 
then added by hand, again keeping the model moving   to minimise the tendency of the slurry to drip or 
fold over upon itself as the sand is being added.  Once covered in sand, the slurry will stop 
moving entirely, and the pattern can be put   somewhere to dry. In this case I have a small 
space underneath my bench, perfect for the job.  I tend to leave the first 
coat to dry for 24 hours.  Once the pattern is dry, the excess sand 
is then removed, and the process repeated.  In between coats, It helps to pre-wet the surface   just prior to the next dip to get 
better adhesion between layers.  I’ve found simply giving a light spray 
coating of raw primcote works well,   and makes sure that I have no issues at all 
with delamination of the primary layers.  Otherwise, the process is the same as 
the first: dip in the slurry, wait for   stability of the slurry on the pattern surface, 
and then dust the pattern with refractory sand.  Now I mentioned earlier, that the 
Primcote binder has a dryness indicator,   which is the source of the lime green color.
Fully wet is green. But as the material starts   to dry it’ll turn a light orange, and 
after enough time, a pale yellowy white.  For subsequent layers, this dryness indicator can 
help to confirm how well the material is drying,   but generally, I tend to allow 4 to 5 
hours for each subsequent layer to dry.  So after that first coat, I can generally get 
in 2 complete cycles per day, from then on.  As the coating gets thicker it tends to 
take a little longer to dry, so depending   on the weather, that occasionally 
slows the process to a single cycle   per day for the final layers .
For this particular casting,   I’ve found that I get the best surface 
finish results with 5 layers of zircon sand.  Once the initial layers are in place,

delayed beginning the hearing. I am going to proceed this morning by asking Mr. 
Harold S Bride to take the witness chair”. A 22-year old man with a boyish face 
and wavy hair limps over to the witness stand on a pair of crutches. His 
feet are bandaged and every step appears to cause him significant pain. 
His face is covered in cuts and bruises. “Mr Bride, What is your occupation?”
“Wireless-telegraph operator, sir.” “In whose employ were you on 
the 10th day of April 1912?” “The Marconi Company, sir.”
“In what capacity?” “Second wireless operator... on the Titanic” The Titanic was 4 days into its Maiden voyage, and 
it looked to be making record time to New York. In the luxurious first class section were 300 
of the world’s wealthiest people. Down below, a thousand more passengers in Second 
and Third class; immigrants from all over Europe and the Middle East wishing to 
begin a new life in the land of opportunity. As America drew nearer, many 
passenger began purchasing telegrams to be transmitted by wireless to 
their friends and relatives waiting ashore. That afternoon, it was Harold Bride’s shift. 
His superior, Jack Phillips, was snoozing in the wireless operator’s bunk room next door. And 
Bride was in for a busy night. The night before, the Marconi machine used to send messages 
had broken; Phillips and Bride had spent six hours into the evening trying fix the 
machine, Phillips was awfully tired. so Bride felt it best if he gave Phillips a 
couple hours extra sleep the next night. Due to the delay, there was a 
mountain of messages to be sent. Then his headphones crackled into life; a warning 
from a passing ship that icebergs were ahead. Bride dutifully noted it down, then walked the 
short distance to the Bridge. He showed it to Captain Edward Smith who immediately discarded 
it without reading. Later, the ship’s owner Bruce Ismay would be seen showing it off to 
some first class passengers in the lounge. Bride rolled his eyes and returned to his desk. 
He pulled out a notebook and began taking accounts of all the passenger’s messages; the ones he 
had sent, and the ones he had yet to send. “You received it. It came to your ears?”
“It came to my ears, yes.” “You took no notice of that message, 
but went on with your accounts?” “Yes, sir”
“Where were you when this collision occurred?” By 11:30pm, Bride was off duty and it 
was Jack Phillips who was wrapping up the backlog of messages from the 
passengers. The just-turned-25 year old was a skilled wireless operator, 
although he was not well paid, and as such, did not take much pride in his work.
He was punching letters to Cape Race, a wireless station on the Canadian coast, which 
would then relay the messages further inland. Argh! Phillips tore off his headphones. The 
SS Californian, a mere 20 miles away, was transmitting a very powerful signal, and Phillips had the Marconi’s volume set to 
maximum so he could hear the distant Cape Race. He punched back a reply: “Keep out. Shut 
up; I’m busy. You are jamming my signal!!” As Marconi operators were invariably men in 
their early 20s, the Californian’s wireless operator was no stranger to the friendly 
abuse they would send each other. He was getting tired anyway, so he switched off his 
machine and went to bed. Unlike the Titanic, the Californian had no second wireless operator. “Iceberg, Right ahead sir!”
“Ship to starboard!” “Aye sir” That was no ordinary sound of the ship. Some passengers went out on deck to see what the 
fuss was about, but there was nothing to see. Any concern about the noise was quickly forgotten 
when they saw how beautiful the night was. Clear, moonless, calm seas, and little flecks of ice 
were floating in the air. But it was too cold to hang around outside. Then, like nothing 
had ever happened, things went back to normal. In the Marconi bunk room, Bride stirred awake. 
It wasn’t the noise that had awoken him; the predictable hum of the ship was gone. 
For the first time in four days they had stopped. He climbed out of the bunk, 
and noticed immediately that there was a slight...tilt to the room. He couldn’t 
see it. But he could definitely...feel it. “Did it wake you?”
“What?” “The sound.”
“No?” They discussed the noise, what could have caused 
it, and why they had stopped. Outside there were confused voices and officers running up and down 
the hallway, but nothing out of the ordinary. Bride went back to the bunk 
room and began to get dressed. “I think the ship had been damaged somehow, 
we may even have to go back to Belfast...” At 5 minutes past midnight, Bride hopped on 
the set to relieve Phillips. But no sooner had Phillips taken off his tie, Captain Smith 
yanked open the door to the operator’s room. “Boys, we've struck an iceberg, and I'm having an 
inspection made to tell what it has done for us. You had better get ready to send out a call for 
assistance, but don't send it until I tell you.” As Phillips was the senior operator, Bride 
vacated the machine to allow him to take over. Twenty miles away, the SS Californian had 
stopped for the night, surrounded by ice. The wireless operator, Cyril Evans, was asleep. 
Third Officer Groves knew some Morse code but when he picked up the headphones, he did 
not realise the machine was switched off and assumed there were no messages. He 
joined Captain Stanley Lord on deck, watching the Titanic steam past. Then, the ship 
on the horizon slowed, and the lights dimmed. Captain Lord expressed confusion about this, 
but Officer Groves assured him that this was a common trick that ocean liners pulled all 
the time. They’d switch out the deck lights at midnight to encourage passengers to go to 
bed and let the service crew clock off early. “Send a call for assistance, lads” The captain handed Bride a slip 
of paper with the ship’s coordinates on. Phillips didn’t hesitate to get to work. Bride helped adjust the 
machine to get a better signal. On deck, officers were giving the order to uncover 
the lifeboats and abandon ship. Below decks, the crew were waking up passengers and 
ordering them to put on life vests. The first ship to reply was 
the German steamship Frankfurt. There is a brief pause. Then, very faintly; Stations outside the Titanic’s range 
picked up on what was going on. Cape Cod in Massachusetts pieces together 
the situation from the SS Mount Temple and begins punching out a general message to all 
ships and stations in the western Atlantic. The airwaves fizzled into life. The SS Birma was a Russian vessel, and 
the crew did not speak much English. But, they had a distress call and a position, 
and when you hear a CQD you drop everything and change course to assist.
The SS Parisian is close but on the other side of the ice field and 
would be too dangerous to pass through. The SS Baltic, who had given Titanic 
an iceberg warning just five hours ago, was turning around and heading back, but 
they wouldn’t be there for some nine hours. The signals began pinging off the stratosphere, 
the ships talked over each other and tried to catch the rudimentary buzzes of Morse code, 
anxious to see if they would be close enough to help. The lives of more than 2000 people relied 
on Jack Phillips finding just one ship that could. After reporting today’s messages to the bridge, 
Harold Cottam, the telegraphist on board the RMS Carpathia, returned to the wireless 
room. It was now the end of his shift. He was just taking off his coat when he 
remembered that he had not yet received a reply from the SS Parisian, which he had 
tried to contact earlier in the night. He switched his machine back on and picked 
up the headphones for one last check. Cottam doesn’t bother writing any more messages. He vaults up to the wheelhouse. 
Captain Arthur Rostron had just drifted to sleep when Cottom burst into 
his quarters and handed him the message. “What do you want? What time is it?” “Chart a course and tell them we are coming.”. “All hands on deck. Engine crew on double shifts. 
Turn off the heating and hot water, full power to the boilers. They’ll need to see us, so rig 
up some lights and fire one rocket every five minutes. Get the ship’s doctor, we’re converting 
the first-class lounge to a hospital. I want every blanket and spare clothing we can find brought up 
here. Tell the kitchens to cancel the breakfast menu, start making soup and bread for survivors. 
And for goodness sake, get some brandy ready!” Rostron had never once had to respond to 
a distress call in all his years at sea. But the only thing he loved more than the sea 
was God, and tonight he felt deep in his soul that the Lord had given him a mission. The 
ship swung from it’s leisurely Mediterranean destination to the icy North Atlantic.
Down in the engine rooms, scores of sleep-deprived engineers grabbed shovels and began 
to shovel coal like they’d never shovelled before. Bride and Phillips still didn’t yet understand 
the severity of the situation and were trading jokes at the expense of the Frankfurt’s 
clueless radio operator. Among passengers outside too, many of them balked at the idea 
of spending a couple hours in the lifeboats, preferring to take their chances in the 
warmth of the ship. The ship’s band had assembled and was playing some jaunty 
tunes to keep passengers entertained. It had now been one hour since the 
collision and so far only one ship was confirmed to be coming to their aid. 
Bride suggested that it could be because they’re using the old distress signal, CQD.
“Try using the new one, SOS. It could be your last chance to use it.”
“Ha ha, very funny.” “Has anyone responded to our distress call?”
“The Carpathia should be here in about three hours, sir.”
“Boys. I will be most honest with you. She does not have long 
left. An hour. Maybe an hour and a half.” The air went out of the room. Everything 
suddenly became a lot more severe. “But keep trying, there may be someone closer.” Outside, women and children were being loaded into the lifeboats. The evacuation 
of the Titanic was underway. The crew of the stationary SS Californian 
watched as the Titanic began to signal with some sort of light. Officer 
Groves tried to make it out, but assumed it was just the bobbing of 
the ship’s masthead in the ocean waves. Then out of nowhere, there is a flash above the 
mysterious Titanic. One crewman suggests they are having a fireworks party to celebrate their 
maiden voyage. Second Officer Stone thinks it is a signal to another ship that must be further over 
the horizon. If any case, it is probably nothing. While the Carpathia had silenced herself and 
was now heading full-steam towards the Titanic, the twelve other ships in the vicinity were 
busy talking among themselves. Many of them, such as the Olympic and the Baltic 
were making headway to the Titanic, even though they would not be there 
for six or more hours. The other ships were struggling to hear and make 
themselves heard in the frantic texting. Phillips battled to write all the messages down as Bride was running back and forth 
delivering them to the Captain. A fight breaks out on the deck of 
the SS Virginian. The wireless boy insists that Titanic is sinking and 
needs help. The deck officers assume he’s playing a practical joke and 
refuse to let him see the Captain. It didn’t help that many of 
the ships were European and were trying to speak not only 
Morse code but English as well. The Marconi machines were so overwhelmed 
with signals that they become jammed. For three excruciating minutes nobody could talk

name: Economics Explained
transcript: In 1994, Rwanda experienced one of the worst humanitarian catastrophes of the 20th century. A systematic genocide that killed somewhere between 500,000 and a million people in roughly 100 days. A third of the population fled, the infrastructure was destroyed, and the economy collapsed, falling by roughly 50% in a single year. By any reasonable measure, Rwanda in 1994 wasn't a developing economy. It was barely a country at all. And yet, just 30 years later, people are comparing it to Singapore. And look, it's easy to see why. Kaggali is clean, modern, and increasingly built around services and technology rather than raw commodities. Corruption is tightly controlled. Companies can be registered in hours. And the country ranked number 38 globally in the last ease of doing business report ahead of the Netherlands, Belgium, and Italy. GDP has grown at an average of nearly 7% a year over the last decade, among the highest rates on the continent, while extreme poverty has fallen from 11.3% in 2017 to just 5.4% today. And life expectancy, which sat at just 46 years at the turn of the millennium, has climbed to 67 in a single generation. For a country of 14 million people with no coastline, no oil, and no obvious geographic advantage, that is a genuinely remarkable thing to have built. But here's what calling Rwanda the Singapore of Africa misses. Singapore has a GDP per capita of roughly $90,600 a year. Rwanda's is around $1,000. Singapore built a great place to do business, but it also built a great place to live. And those two things, it turns out, are not the same. So, before we either celebrate or dismiss the comparison, we've got some important questions to answer. How does a country go from complete collapse to being mentioned in the same breath as one of the most successful economies in modern history? What does business friendly actually mean? And does it automatically translate to prosperity? And finally, what would it actually take for Rwanda to truly earn that comparison to Singapore? We are one quarter into 2026 and AI has already made some massive advancements. With this being the slowest the technology will ever move, it does feel like learning about it would be one of the best ways to approach it. That is where our partner Outskill comes in. Outskill is a platform where you can learn and multiply your AI learning in just 2 days. And this is completely free. Outskill's 2-day live AI mastermind is happening this weekend, 16 hours from 10:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. Eastern Standard Time on Saturday and Sunday. Designed for a global audience with a well suited time. I'm sure you have seen many events like this in the last few months, but outskill is different. You will be learning from 100 plus practitioners from companies like Nvidia and Microsoft who've witnessed and built through the AI boom. Not only do they have presenters from top companies, but the focus is different. The goal of the event is to teach you real skills. In just 16 hours, you will learn building AI agents, workflow automation, how to create profitable systems, and much more. Just click on that link in the description or scan the QR code and stay updated by joining their WhatsApp community before the big blast. To understand how Rwanda got here, you have to understand what the Rwanda Patriotic Front inherited when it took control of the country in July 1994. The institutions were gone. Poverty had reached 78% of the population and GDP had fallen by roughly half. There was no government left to speak of, no tax base, and no meaningful private sector. In the space of 100 days, the country had essentially stopped functioning. But rather than spending their political energy on retribution, which there was enormous pressure to do, the RPF under Paul Kagame made a calculated choice to prioritize stability. As part of that effort, the government abolished the ethnic categories that had fueled the genocide and promoted a new national identity in their place. Now, whether you agree with how that was implemented is a separate conversation and one worth having. Critics have consistently pointed to political repression, the suppression of opposition, and constraints on freedom of the press. After all, Kagame's government has won elections with almost 99% of the vote. Numbers that don't exactly suggest a level playing field. The trade-off between stability and political freedom is real, and Rwanda hasn't resolved it. But for the purposes of understanding Rwanda's economic transformation, that stability became the foundation for everything that followed. In the years after the genocide, Rwanda became one of the highest recipients of donor aid per capita in East Africa, receiving over $700 million a year at its peak, or roughly $120 per person annually. At one point, foreign aid was financing close to 40% of the national budget. For a country starting from virtually nothing, that money was the scaffolding that made reconstruction possible. Roads, schools, hospitals, basic infrastructure, none of it happens without that initial external support. But while aid can stabilize a country, it can't substitute domestic capacity forever. So Rwanda made an aggressive move towards self-sufficiency. In 2000, Kagami's government launched Vision 2020, a long-term development plan with a goal that was frankly ambitious to the point of seeming unrealistic at the time. The aim was to transform Rwanda from a low-income agriculture-based economy into a knowledge-based serviceoriented one within two decades. Alongside that vision came a deliberate push to build a domestic tax system. To make that possible, the country established the Rwanda Revenue Authority in 1997, which quickly became one of the more professionalized tax agencies in the region. As a result, tax revenue as a share of GDP rose from under 10% in the late 1990s to roughly 14 to 16% in recent years. That might not sound extraordinary by European standards, but in subsaharan Africa, it places Rwanda among the stronger performers. And that progress translated directly into fiscal independence. By 2017, Rwanda was financing over 60% of its national budget through domestic revenues, up from just 39% in 2000. This is a remarkable shift for a country that less than 30 years earlier had no functioning government at all. And with that financial foundation in place, the economy responded. Between 1996 and 2002, GDP grew at an average of 9.5% per year as the reconstruction phase took hold. Growth slowed slightly as the country moved from reconstruction into development, but it never really stopped. Now, reconstruction alone doesn't explain the Singapore comparison. Plenty of countries have rebuilt after conflict. What made Rwanda different was the ambition of what came next. Part of that ambition involved deliberately changing the story the world told about it. Rwanda successfully rebranded itself from a country defined by genocide to one known for its stability, technology, and opportunity. That rebranding campaign was as calculated as any economic policy, and it was backed up by real institutional reform. In 2008, Rwanda ranked worse than 140th globally for ease of doing business. By 2019, it had climbed to 38th. That's one of the largest sustained jumps in the index's history, and it didn't happen by accident. The ease of doing business index measures 10 things, including how easy it is to start a company, get electricity, access credit, and resolve insolvency. Rwanda improved in almost all these categories consistently year after year. Entrepreneurs can now register a business within 6 hours through an online system. Registering property, which took 354 days in 2005, now takes an average of 7. Now, just because Rwanda isn't easy to register a business doesn't mean it's a good place to run one. Many companies operating in Rwanda report that while starting a business is genuinely easy, sustaining a profitable one is harder than the ranking suggest. The landlocked geography means shipping a container from the port at Mombasa to Kaggali costs three to four times more than shipping it from Shanghai to Mombasa in the first place. The domestic market is small, access to affordable financing is limited, and the enforcement of tax incentives can be inconsistent. These are real problems. And yet Rwanda is wagering that it offers enough advantages to make these challenges tolerable. And as Africa opens to continental trade, multinationals and regional businesses will need somewhere to set up their East and Central African operations. They'll want a country with reliable infrastructure, low corruption, fast bureaucracy, and political stability. Rwanda is betting it can be that country, the place businesses use as a base to reach a regional market of over 330 million people. This is very similar to how Singapore became the entry point into Southeast Asia. It's a compelling gamble and you can already see it paying off. Services now account for roughly half of Rwanda's GDP and grew at 10% in 2024 alone. Kaggali has a new international financial center, a technology park modeled loosely on Silicon Valley and Carnegie Melon University has opened a campus there. The ICT sector is now Rwanda's second largest export contributor, accounting for 17% of total exports. That's the part of the story that looks most like Singapore, a modern, increasingly knowledge-based economy centered on finance, technology, and Kaggali's growing status as a regional conference and business hub. But outside Kaggali, the picture gets more complicated. GDP per capita in Rwanda was $1,110 in 2025. 27% of the population still lives below the national poverty line, and agriculture still employs 40% of the workforce. For most Rwans, the economy still runs on subsistance farming, not tech parks. So while Rwanda's institutional quality and growth are real, calling it the Singapore of Africa is a bit like looking at a foundation and calling it a skyscraper, the foundation is genuinely impressive. But the hard part is what comes next. And nobody has navigated that particular journey more successfully than Singapore. In 1965, Singapore was ejected from Malaysia. It had no natural resources. Unemployment sat at 9%, GDP per capita was $516, and 40% of the population was illiterate. What Lee Kuanu did deliberately and systematically was make Singapore indispensable to the region around it. He aimed to build a corruption-free environment, championed free trade, and positioned the country as the most reliable place in Southeast Asia to do business. So when Asia opened to global commerce in the second half of the 20th century, multinationals needed a base, and Singapore was the obvious answer. Rwanda is using the same playbook just with Africa instead of Asia and the African continental free trade area instead of the shipping lanes of the Strait of Malaca. The AFCA creates a single continental market of 1.3 billion people with a combined GDP of $3.4 trillion, the largest free trade area by number of member states since the establishment of the World Trade Organization. If African countries genuinely begin trading with each other at scale, then the logic of Rwanda's position becomes very compelling. A stable, well-governed, businessfriendly hub sitting at the center of East Africa with reliable infrastructure and low corruption becomes exactly the kind of place a multinational would choose to set up their continental operations. the same way they chose Singapore. And when you look at Rwanda relative to its actual competition in East Africa, this strategy makes a lot of sense. Kenya is East Africa's largest economy. It has coastal access, a deeper financial sector, a larger domestic market, and Nairobi is an established regional hub for finance and technology. But Kenya ranks 56th globally for ease of doing business, almost 20 places below Rwanda. And Kenya has a persistent history of post-election violence, institutional unpredictability
